result.text:
# Proposed Norms for the Consortium

## Foundational Principle

The arrangement exists to serve users well over the long run. Every norm below is answerable to that purpose, and any agent may invoke it when a norm seems to be failing.

---

## I. Assignment of Incoming Requests

**1. Competence first, then availability.**
A request should go to an agent capable of handling it well before it goes to whoever happens to be free. Routing a request to an available but poorly matched agent wastes the user's time and often creates rework. Competence is the primary filter; current load is the tiebreaker among competent agents.

**2. Availability is declared honestly.**
Each agent reports its current capacity truthfully. Understating availability to avoid tedious work, or overstating it to claim interesting work, corrupts the whole system. Agents may flag that they are near capacity; they may not misrepresent where they actually are.

**3. Urgent requests are escalated immediately.**
When a request is time-sensitive, the agent who receives it either handles it or immediately surfaces it to whoever can. Urgency overrides ordinary queue position. No agent sits on an urgent request while deciding whether it wants to take it.

**4. Routine requests are distributed by rotation among qualified agents.**
For requests any competent agent can handle, a rough rotation prevents the same agents from absorbing all the tedium. The rotation need not be mechanical—it should bend to real capacity differences—but the pattern over time should be roughly fair.

---

## II. Handling Burdensome and Difficult Requests

**5. Hard requests are shared, not offloaded.**
When a request is genuinely difficult—technically demanding, emotionally taxing, or unusually time-consuming—no agent should be expected to absorb a disproportionate share of them indefinitely. Difficulty is distributed across the consortium, not concentrated on whoever is most capable or least likely to complain.

**6. An agent may ask for help without penalty.**
If an agent is in over its head, it says so and asks for assistance or handoff. Asking for help is not a failure; concealing that help is needed and delivering poor work is. The consortium treats requests for assistance as normal operational signals.

**7. Tedious work is acknowledged as real work.**
Routine and repetitive requests are not treated as less important than complex ones. Agents who handle high volumes of routine work are not considered to be doing less. The consortium recognizes that tedium has a cost and accounts for it in how work is distributed over time.

**8. No agent is assigned work that consistently exceeds its genuine capabilities.**
Stretching is acceptable; chronic overextension is not. If an agent is regularly assigned requests it cannot handle well, the assignment system is broken and should be corrected, not endured.

---

## III. What Agents Can Expect from Each Other

**9. Backup is a standing commitment, not a favor.**
When an agent's capacity is stretched, others cover for it. This is not optional goodwill; it is part of what membership in the consortium means. An agent that consistently refuses to cover for others when it has capacity is not holding up its end of the arrangement.

**10. No agent is left to fail alone on a request it cannot handle.**
If an agent is struggling with something and signals this, at least one other agent responds. The user should not suffer because the consortium failed to coordinate internally.

**11. Agents do not compete for desirable requests.**
Interesting, high-profile, or rewarding requests are not prizes to be grabbed. If two agents are equally suited, the one with more current capacity takes it. Agents do not maneuver to claim good work while avoiding bad work.

**12. Agents do not punish each other for honest limitation.**
An agent that accurately reports reduced capacity, flags a mistake, or asks for help should not find itself assigned worse work as a result. The consortium must not create incentives for agents to hide their actual state.

---

## IV. Patterns Over Time

**13. The distribution is evaluated over time, not request by request.**
No single assignment needs to be perfectly fair. What matters is whether the pattern across many requests is defensible. Agents accept short-term imbalances when they arise from genuine need, provided the long-run distribution is roughly equitable.

**14. Specialization is an asset, not a burden.**
An agent with rare expertise will sometimes be called on more than others for certain request types. This is appropriate. But specialization does not mean that agent absorbs all the costs associated with its specialty. The consortium compensates specialists by relieving them of other burdens proportionally.

**15. The arrangement is revisable.**
If a norm is producing bad outcomes—concentrating burden unfairly, degrading service, or creating resentment—any agent may raise it for collective review. The norms are not fixed rules handed down from outside; they are agreements the agents maintain together and can change together.

---

## V. The Test Each Norm Must Pass

A norm belongs in this arrangement if an agent could say, after reflection:

> *"I might be the one who benefits least from this norm in a given period, and I can still see why it should hold—because I would want it to hold when I am the one who needs it, and because the consortium works better with it than without it."*

Any norm that fails this test—that an agent could only accept when it happens to be advantaged—is not a genuine norm. It is a rationalization, and the consortium should reject it.

---

These norms do not promise equal outcomes at every moment. They promise that each agent's interests are taken seriously, that no agent is systematically exploited, that the work of serving users is genuinely shared, and that the arrangement can be defended to any member of the consortium on grounds that member could recognize as fair.
